# NeurIPS readiness assessment

*Generated by research-copilot for advisor review. Honest take on
where the paper currently stands, what reviewers will and won't buy,
and the smallest set of experiments that meaningfully moves it from
"borderline" to "competitive at main track".*

## Bottom line

The work is at a strong **NeurIPS Datasets & Benchmarks track** level
*today*. After the in-flight cross-model and cross-harness panels
land, it is **borderline-low for the NeurIPS main track**. The
strengths are real, but the methodology-novelty bar at main is
unforgiving for application work, and the strategy of "rigorous
empirical analysis to compensate for modest methodological novelty"
is the right strategy but isn't fully airtight without a few specific
additions.

The decision the advisor should make is **venue choice**:
- **D&B track** plays to the existing strengths and likely accepts.
- **Main track** requires either accepting borderline-low odds, or
  spending another 1–2 weeks on the specific reach experiments
  enumerated below.

## What is genuinely strong

### 1. The bottleneck-analysis pipeline is the most transferable contribution
Most applied-agent papers stop at "we ran an ablation, X helps".
This work decomposes aggregate quality differences into per-task,
per-cell failure-mode counts via a two-stage LLM pipeline:
\texttt{deepseek-v4-flash} classifies each (cell, seed, task) into
8 structural failure categories with strict-JSON output;
\texttt{deepseek-v4-pro} synthesises the per-cell distributions into
a paper-grade narrative anchored on a chosen baseline/best pair.
Total cost ${\sim}\$5\text{--}7$ across ${\sim}650$ classifications;
the methodology is reusable on any benchmark with structured ground
truth. Reviewers who recognise this will see methodological
substance beyond the GEOS application.

### 2. The Resolution-IV $2^{4-1}$ factorial is unusually rigorous
Most application papers run ad-hoc one-factor-at-a-time ablations.
This paper uses a Resolution-IV fractional factorial with generator
$\mathrm{D}{=}\mathrm{ABC}$, recovering all four main effects free of
two-factor confounding. That is design-of-experiments done correctly.
The $\mathrm{S+X+M}$ "factorial completion" cell at the predicted
main-effects-best corner is a thoughtful addition.

### 3. Negative results worth reporting
Three findings that reviewers will recognise as honest and useful:
- The agent never invokes the memory-as-retrieval MCP tool — pure
  procedural-memory-as-retrieval doesn't work for this task-model
  pair. Most applied-agent papers don't catch this kind of negative.
- RAG (the R factor) is the only main effect that clears noise, and
  it is **negative** on test-17.
- The number of strictly-perfect decks does *not* increase under any
  adapter ($7/51$ Vanilla, $6/51$ X+M, $6/51$ SE). The harness
  operates in a harm-reduction regime, not a correctness regime.

This last finding is sharp and generalisable beyond GEOS: it is a
testable claim about the limits of schema-validation-style adapters
for any structured-generation task.

### 4. The benchmark itself
A 17+10-task GEOS XML authoring benchmark with tree-aware structural
similarity scoring under failures-as-zero aggregation. Geophysics
multiphysics has been bypassed at top ML venues to date, and the
benchmark is the first such evaluation. Both the metric (TreeSim,
recursive subtree-weighted alignment) and the failure-as-zero
aggregation policy are appropriate to the task.

### 5. The OOD→hard-tail framing walk-back, done correctly, is a strength
The paper originally claimed an in-distribution-vs-OOD effect; we
caught that the gap is concentrated in 3 of 10 tasks where the
baseline catastrophically fails (TreeSim $< 0.7$). The 7
non-pathological Held-out-10 tasks score $0.898$ on average,
indistinguishable from test-17's $0.910$. Reframing this as a
"hard-tail" rather than an "OOD" effect is the correct read. Most
papers would not catch their own framing weakness; reviewers who
notice the careful framing reward it.

## What hurts main-track odds

### 1. Single backbone for the headline (even after cross-model)
All factorial cells run on \texttt{deepseek-v4-flash}. The cross-model
panel will add \texttt{minimax-m2.7} and \texttt{google/gemini-3-flash-preview}
on Vanilla / X+M / SE only, single-seed. Reviewers will ask: "would
you have made the same X+M $\geq$ S+X+M $\geq$ SE recommendation on
Llama-405B or Qwen-72B?" and the answer will be "we don't know".

### 2. Method-novelty critique is real
R, S, X, M are individually existing techniques: domain RAG plugins
(many), Stop-hooks (Claude Code-native), schema-validation MCP tools
(emerging), distilled memory cheatsheets (Buffer-of-Thoughts and
similar). The novelty argument has to lean entirely on (a) the
factorial design and (b) the bottleneck-analysis pipeline. Both are
defensible, but Reviewer #2 *will* write something like "interesting
empirical study, but the ML contribution is unclear".

### 3. TreeSim is structural, not physical
TreeSim measures tree-edit similarity to ground truth. It does not
measure whether the deck actually compiles, runs, or produces correct
physics. A fair reviewer asking "can the agent's deck pass GEOS's own
parser, or run a single timestep?" lands a hit we currently have no
answer to.

### 4. No competitive baselines from peer domain-agents
The paper cites Foam-Agent, MetaOpenFOAM, ChemCrow, etc. as related
work but does not compare directly. A reviewer will ask: "what does
a port of MetaOpenFOAM-style architecture achieve on this benchmark?"
We have no head-to-head data.

### 5. Hard-tail finding is concentrated in 3 tasks
The headline +6.9pp Vanilla→SE gain on Held-out-10 decomposes into
two large rescues (ThermoPoroElasticWellbore +0.406, ProppantTest
+0.284) plus one universal failure (HydraulicFracture, all cells
score $0.013$). Effect-size claims off $n{=}2{-}3$ rescued tasks are
fragile. A reviewer with an empirical bent will press on this.

### 6. The Brian-merge reproducibility caveat
Two upstream commits by a collaborator landed on remote `main`
during the final hour of the scaleup phase, then got pulled-rebased
onto the `[CAMPAIGN]` commit. The actual experiments ran on the
pre-merge tree (now tagged `autocamp-experiment-state`). We resolved
this with an env-flag gate so default behaviour reproduces the
experiment-state harness, but it adds a footnote of the form
"results reproduce when env var X is unset". This is honest, but it
is friction reviewers don't usually expect to deal with.

### 7. The hygiene-audit and pivot subsection
The paper currently includes a "What changed in our narrative"
section disclosing an early memory-index leak (13 of 17 test-task
basenames in auxiliary fields) that the hygiene gate now catches.
This is the right thing to disclose, but it provides reviewers with
explicit ammunition.

## What would meaningfully move main-track odds

In roughly decreasing impact:

1. **Run a sample of agent-produced decks through actual GEOS
   execution** — even just one or two tasks per cell, reporting
   "X of Y converge under simulation". This converts TreeSim from a
   proxy into a ladder. Probably the single biggest reach experiment
   for the cost.

2. **Build the cross-section consistency hook the bottleneck analysis
   identified** (validate every \texttt{<ElementRegion materialList>}
   entry exists as a \texttt{name=} in \texttt{<Constitutive>}), run
   it as a new cell, show it reduces \texttt{bad\_attribute\_value}
   failures. This converts the bottleneck-analysis-as-observation
   into bottleneck-analysis-as-design-tool.

3. **One competitive baseline from a port of a peer domain-agent**
   (e.g. a Foam-Agent-style architecture re-implemented for GEOS).
   Even a rough port — enough to show "we are not just better than
   nothing".

4. **Cross-model and cross-harness panels filled in** — necessary,
   but not sufficient on their own. They address the single-backbone
   critique without addressing the deeper method-novelty critique.

5. **Larger Held-out-10 with deliberate hard-task sampling** — adds
   3-5 more compound multi-physics tasks (geothermal, induced
   seismicity, hydraulic-fracture-with-leakoff) to give the hard-tail
   finding more $n$.

What I would *not* do at this point: more polish on the existing
analysis. The methodology is already rigorous; the missing piece is
reach.

## Three concrete recommendations

### Path A — D&B track (safer, higher acceptance probability)

Submit to the NeurIPS Datasets & Benchmarks track with the current
results plus the in-flight cross-model and cross-harness panels.
Frame the contribution as: a multiphysics-simulator XML authoring
benchmark, a TreeSim metric with structural decomposition, and a
bottleneck-analysis pipeline that decomposes adapter wins into
specific structural failure modes. The hard-tail finding becomes a
benchmark insight rather than an effect-size claim.

This is the path I would take if optimising for acceptance.

### Path B — Main track with reach experiments (1–2 weeks more work)

Add at the minimum:
- The execution-correctness check on a subset of decks
- The new adapter cell informed by the bottleneck analysis (cross-
  section consistency hook)
- One peer-agent baseline (even rough)

These three additions, plus the cross-model and cross-harness panels
already in flight, would convert the paper from "rigorous application
study" into "rigorous application study with new adapter design and
peer comparison". That is enough to move from borderline-low to
borderline-medium at main.

### Path C — Workshop (fastest, weakest)

ML4PS, Agents4Science, or AI for Science. Acceptance is essentially
guaranteed, but the venue prestige is lower. Reasonable as a backup
if neither A nor B fits the timeline.

## What I am about to do regardless of the path

The cross-model and cross-harness panels are useful for any of the
three paths and are already planned. Specifically:
- Cross-model on Claude Code: Vanilla, X+M, SE × \{minimax-m2.7,
  gemini-3-flash-preview\} × test-17, single seed (cost-driven).
- Cross-harness on OpenHands: Vanilla and X+M-equivalent × DSv4-flash
  × test-17 × 3 seeds.
- OpenCode dropped for v1.

Default harness state: \texttt{GEOS\_HOOK\_POSTTOOLUSE} unset, so the
new runs are directly comparable to the autocamp numbers in the
paper.

## My honest one-line summary for the advisor

The paper has the rigour reviewers want; it is missing the **scope**
they expect at main. Either narrow scope to a venue that fits
(D\&B / workshop) or expand scope (execution evaluation, new adapter
from the analysis, peer-agent baseline) for main.
